hook: The agent hype cycle just hit a Pi 4 running at 3W.
intro_context: 2025 was full of agent demos that needed cloud GPUs. By mid-2026, at least four open-source projects let you run real agentic workflows on a Raspberry Pi 4 or 5 — vector memory, tool execution, multi-agent orchestration, all local. MolluskAI, 9Lives, OpenClaw, and Be More Agent all shipped within months of each other.
three_key_points: MolluskAI runs a full agent pipeline on a Pi 4 with vector memory, Telegram gateway, web search, and scheduled tasks — all routed through OpenRouter for under 3k tokens per call.
9Lives brings multi-agent orchestration (Crews + Lanes) to commodity ARM hardware with a Docker compose deploy on a Pi 5.
Be More Agent proves you can run wake-word + STT + LLM + TTS + vision all on-device on a single Pi 5 with zero cloud dependency.
three_beat_structure: Hook: Four projects shipped agent stacks that run on a Pi this year. That did not exist last year.
Payoff: Side-by-side of three agent startup times and first-response latency on an 8GB Pi 5.
CTA: Clone the one that matches your stack and run it tonight.
demo_segment: SSH into a Pi 5 running MolluskAI. Show the Telegram chat log with a web search query routed through the agent, then a scheduled task firing a disk usage report. Clock the memory footprint with `htop`. Switch to 9Lives and show the Crew orchestration panel dispatching a summarization task across two agents.
caveats: These projects are new. Documentation varies wildly. MolluskAI needs an OpenRouter API key — it is not fully local. 9Lives Docker image is 1.2GB. Be More Agent requires Pi 5 for the vision pipeline. None of these are production-grade yet. Seed agent is fascinating but runs on Claude CLI via OAuth, not a local model.
closing_takeaway: The gap between agent vapor and a real `docker compose up` on a Pi closed this year. Practical agents are here — they just look like bash scripts and Telegram bots, not sci-fi demos.
call_to_action: Pick one project from this post, run it on your Pi tonight, and report your first bottleneck back.
short_script: Four agent projects shipped for Raspberry Pi this year and I tested all of them. MolluskAI runs a Telegram-connected agent with vector memory and scheduled tasks on a Pi 4. Nine Lives lets you orchestrate multiple agents with Docker on a Pi 5. Be More Agent does wake word, vision, speech, and LLM all on device. None of this existed twelve months ago. The catch is none are production ready yet and documentation is hit or miss. Still worth testing tonight.
visual_idea: Split screen showing three SSH terminals each running a different agent project, synced to show startup time.
cta: Clone one, run it on your Pi, post the bottleneck.

suggested_titles.curiosity: The year AI agents finally ran on a Raspberry Pi
suggested_titles.practical: Four agent stacks that ship on Pi hardware today
suggested_titles.contrarian: Stop waiting for AGI — ship a useful agent on your Pi tonight
suggested_titles.tutorial: How to deploy a local AI agent on a Raspberry Pi 4 or 5

broll_list: Side-by-side SSH terminals running molluskai, 9Lives, and be-more-agent startup sequences
Telegram chat log showing an agent responding to a web search query, then a scheduled task firing
9Lives Crew orchestration dashboard dispatching summarization across two Lives
insight: The bottleneck for practical agents was never the model — it was the runtime. Once open-source tooling shipped Dockerfiles and one-line installers for ARM, the floodgates opened. Distribution wins again.
hooks: Four agent projects shipped for Raspberry Pi this year. None existed twelve months ago.
The most practical AI agent of 2026 runs on a $35 board and costs pennies a day in power.
Agent frameworks finally ship as Docker compose files instead of white papers.
